query: What are the requirements and fees required to submit a valid interconnection application for a large generator in ISO New England, based on the latest ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)?
final_answer:
Based on the ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT), Schedule 22 — Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP), effective May 3, 2025 (FERC ER25-2149-000), and the Interconnection Process Guide, here is a comprehensive answer. --- ## Scope: What Is a "Large Generator"? Schedule 22 applies to **Large Generating Facilities**, which are generating facilities **larger than 20 MW**. Facilities ≤20 MW are subject to Schedule 23 (Small Generator Interconnection Procedures). *(Source: FERC Order on Compliance, Docket Nos. ER24-2009-000 and ER24-2007-000, at P 5; ISO-NE Interconnection Service page)* --- ## 1. Prerequisites Before Submitting Before you can submit a valid interconnection request, you must: 1. **Obtain an ISO Customer ID** — New customers must first obtain a customer ID and access to Critical Energy Infrastructure Information (CEII). 2. **Create an IRTT Account** — Register for the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT). 3. **Submit During a Cluster Request Window** — Per FERC Order No. 2023, IRs can only be submitted during a **45-calendar-day Cluster Request Window**. The next window is expected **October 5, 2026 – November 19, 2026**. *(Source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide)* --- ## 2. Required Components of a Valid Interconnection Request Section 3.4 of Schedule 22 (LGIP) governs what constitutes a Valid Interconnection Request. The following must be submitted via IRTT during the Cluster Request Window *(Sources: Schedule 22 LGIP §3.4; Appendix 1 – Interconnection Request form)*: ### A. Completed Interconnection Request Form (Appendix 1) The form requires: | Information Required | Details | |---|---| | **Project Name** | Proposed project identifier | | **Request Type** | Check one: New Large Generating Facility; Capacity increase/modification (potential Material Modification); New wholesale market participation by existing facility; Change from NR to CNR service | | **Interconnection Service Type** | ☐ Network Resource (NR) – energy only
☐ Capacity Network Resource (CNR) – energy + capacity
☐ Option to downgrade to NR if thermal violations found in CNR testing | | **Facility Address/Location** | Town/City, County, and State | | **Point of Interconnection** | Requested POI | | **Type of Facility** | Description | | **Electric Storage** | Whether facility includes storage; whether it charges from the Administered Transmission System; charging and reactive capability specifications; primary frequency response range for storage | | **Fuel Type** | Generating Facility Fuel Type | | **Capacity by Temperature** | Max Gross MW, Max Net MW, and Net MW at POI for temps: ≥90°F, ≥50°F, ≥20°F, ≥0°F | | **Requested Service Levels (MW)** | CNR Summer/Winter; NR Summer/Winter | | **Equipment Configuration** | Description including number of units and GSUs, any control technologies to restrict output | | **Dates** | Requested Commercial Operation Date, Initial Synchronization Date, In-Service Date | | **Site Control Evidence** | See Section 2.C below | | **Customer Information** | Company name, ISO Customer ID, address, representative name/title/contact | | **Authorized Signature** | Signed by an officer of the company | ### B. Required Deposits and Fees The application must be accompanied by deposits. Per Schedule 22 LGIP §3.1 and the ISO-NE Order No. 2023 compliance presentation: | Deposit Type | Amount | Notes | |---|---|---| | **Study Deposit** | **$250,000** (uniform amount per ISO-NE independent entity variation) | Submitted with IR. Consistent with existing New England LGIP requirements. Can be by electronic transfer or irrevocable Letter of Credit from an approved issuer. *(Sources: ISO-NE Order No. 2023 presentation, Sept 2023, slide 14; ISO-NE Transition Q&A, Dec 2023, Q16)* | | **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)** | Required at IR submission (amount as specified per cluster study process) | Must be delivered by electronic transfer or irrevocable Letter of Credit. If LOC is deficient, customer has 5 business days to cure. *(Source: Schedule 22 LGIP §3.1, as reflected in Feb 2024 tariff redlines)* | ### C. Site Control Requirements Per **Schedule 22 LGIP §3.4.2** and **Appendix 1** and the **June 2025 ISO-NE Technical Notice on Regulatory Limitations**: **For Capacity Network Resource (CNR) Interconnection Service:** - **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name is **required — no exceptions**. No deposit-in-lieu option. **For Network Resource (NR) Interconnection Service:** - **Option A:** Provide **100% exclusive Site Control** documentation (fee simple ownership, leasehold, option to purchase, contract to purchase, or applications for permits on federal/state property). - **Option B (if Regulatory Limitations prevent obtaining Site Control):** Provide ALL of the following: 1. **Cash deposit in lieu of Site Control** = **$10,000/MW**, subject to a **minimum of $500,000** and a **maximum of $2,000,000** (refundable within the cure period per §3.4.3) 2. **Signed affidavit from an officer** stating Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations 3. **Documentation** describing and explaining the source and effects of the Regulatory Limitations, including conditions to satisfy them and anticipated timeline **Definition of Regulatory Limitation:** A federal, state, local, or Tribal law that makes it infeasible to obtain Site Control before submitting an IR (e.g., a law requiring a validated ISO-NE IR before obtaining land rights). This does **not** include general permitting or siting issues. *(Source: ISO-NE Technical Notice, June 2, 2025)* **Modifications to existing facilities:** If the proposed modification does not require additional real property, the customer certifies it has Site Control and no additional evidence is needed. ### D. Technical Data Interconnection Customers must submit study-ready technical data as required for the Cluster Study. ISO-NE provides an "Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance" document for this purpose. *(Source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide)* --- ## 3. The ISO Validation Process (Section 3.4.4) Within **5 business days** of receipt, ISO-NE will: - Notify the customer that the IR is valid, **OR** - Identify any deficiencies (including Site Control issues) If deficiencies are identified, the customer has a cure period to remedy them before the Cluster Request Window closes. --- ## 4. Summary of All Required Fees/Deposits Throughout the Process Beyond the initial submission, the LGIP requires escalating financial commitments as the project advances: | Stage | Deposit/ Fee | Amount | |---|---|---| | **IR Submission** | Study Deposit | **$250,000** (uniform) | | **IR Submission** | Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD) | As specified | | **IR Submission (NR only, if no Site Control)** | Site Control Deposit in lieu | **$10,000/MW** (min $500K, max $2M) | | **After Cluster Study Report Meeting** | Additional CRD | **5% of assigned Network Upgrade costs** (within 20 CD) | | **Facilities Study Agreement Execution** | Commercial Readiness Deposit | **Greater of 25% of estimated FS cost or $250,000**, plus proof of **100% Site Control** | | **Interconnection Agreement (LGIA) Execution** | LGIA Deposit | **20% of Network Upgrade costs**, less readiness deposits already on hand | *(Sources: Appendix 3 – Interconnection Facilities Study Agreement §5.0; ISO-NE Oct 2023 cluster study cycle presentation; FERC Order on Compliance ER24-2009-000)* --- ## 5. Key Governing Documents The complete rules are set forth in: - **Schedule 22 of the ISO-NE OATT** — Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (effective 5/3/25) - **ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6 (PP 5-6)** — Scope of Study for Interconnection Studies (effective 10/11/25) - **ISO Operating Procedure No. 14 (OP-14)** — Technical Requirements for Generators - **Appendix 1** – Interconnection Request form - **Appendix 2** – Cluster Study Agreement - **Appendix 3** – Interconnection Facilities Study Agreement - **Appendix 11** – Standard Large Generator Interconnection Agreement (LGIA) All sources: ISO-NE OATT Schedule 22 (iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf); ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide; ISO-NE Appendix 1 IR Form; ISO-NE Technical Notice on Regulatory Limitations (June 2, 2025); FERC Order Docket Nos. ER24-2009-000 and ER24-2007-000 (April 4, 2025).

      "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window.       "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. You must enter into an IA before you can construct or operate a generator or an elective transmission upgrade. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue. (Log in not necessary.) For information on the public version of the ISO New England Interconnection (ISO Queue), visit the Interconnection Request Queue webpage.\n- See the ISO Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) and IRTT User Guide.\n- See the Interconnection Request Studies page to view completed studies for projects listed in the ISO’s Interconnection Request Queue (IR CEII required to view).\n- Interconnection frequently asked questions can be found in Ask ISO.\n- See Order No. 2023 Key Project page to access documents and resources related to the Order No. 2023: Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements.\n- Read ISO-NE’s September 30, 2025, responses to Order Nos. 2023/2023-A transition-related questions\n- Read the Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance document for guidance on submitting the technical data required for a Cluster Study.\n\n## Questions\n\nEmail IRTT Participant Support at irtt@iso-ne.com, or contact ISO Participant Support.\n\n## Interconnection Documents\n\n### Filters\n\nFilter this list ↑\n\n### Documents\n\n| A listing of links to documents. | Title and Description | Date | Type | Size |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n\nSorry, no results are available with this choice of filter(s). Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff. Known as the ISO Tariff, this document details: the rates, terms, and conditions for transmission, market, and other services provided by ISO New England; the rights and responsibilities of the ISO and market participants; and various schedules that define the revenues the ISO collects for its operations and those of the New England States Committee on Electricity (NESCOE).\n\nThe ISO Tariff, in conjunction with the ISO’s Transmission Operating Agreements, also helps satisfy FERC requirements for the ISO’s functioning as a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO).\n\n#### Section I–General Terms and Conditions\n\nThis section includes:\n\n- General Terms and Conditions(10/07/2025)—defined terms and certain rights, privileges, and obligations of all parties, and actions they may or must take under the ISO Tariff\n- Exhibit IA—The Financial Assurance Policy(09/01/2025)\n- Exhibit IB(08/10/2017)—reserved for future use\n- Exhibit IC(08/10/2017)—reserved for future use\n- Exhibit ID—The Billing Policy(02/20/2023)\n\n#### Section II—Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT)\n\nVisit the OATT page. The OATT:\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have fair and open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers, as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n- Sets out the cost recovery and allocation mechanisms for transmission and ancillary services in the region\n\n#### Section III—Market Rule 1\n\nVisit the Market Rule 1 page. Market Rule 1 governs the operation of New England’s wholesale electricity markets. It includes detailed information on pricing, scheduling, offering, bidding, settlement, and other procedures related to the purchase and sale of electricity.\n\n#### Section IV—ISO Funding Mechanisms\n\nThis section details how ISO New England collects funds to pay for its functioning.\n\n- Section IV.A—Recovery of ISO Administrative Expenses(01/01/2026) is known as the Self-Funding Tariff. It contains rates for the administrative functions of the ISO, including costs for scheduling and administering the movement of power through, out of, or within the balancing authority area; and costs for services the ISO provides to administer the energy and reliability markets. The collection of monies for NESCOE operation is also detailed, because the ISO acts as its billing and collection agent.\n- Section IV.B—Capital Funding Arrangements Tariff(01/01/2014) describes how the ISO funds capital projects.\n\n### Attachments to the ISO Tariff\n\n#### Attachment A—Market Participant Service Agreement: (06/17/2013)\n\nThis agreement provides the rights and responsibilities of businesses or organizations that generate, buy, sell, and transport electricity in the New England markets.\n\n#### Attachment A-1: (06/15/2013)\n\nThis attachment is reserved for future filings.\n\n#### Attachment B—Pro Forma ITC Operating Agreement: (08/30/2010)\n\nThis attachment is reserved for future filings.\n\n#### Attachment D—ISO New England Information Policy: (07/26/2024)\n\nThis policy provides the rules and procedures the ISO and stakeholders follow in handling the information collected and created in the administration of New England’s wholesale electricity markets and the operation of the region’s transmission grid. See the List of Authorized Persons and Authorized Commissions per the Information Policy.\n\n#### Attachment E—Service Agreements: (06/17/2013)\n\nThis attachment provides forms for service agreements under the ISO Tariff.\n\n#### Attachment F—Coordination Agreements: (01/01/2025)\n\nThese agreements document the ISO’s coordination of operations with operators of neighboring control areas and the terms for pricing emergency energy and security energy. Other available agreements document the relationship among asset owners of the facilities subject to such coordination agreements.\n\n- ISO-NE/NYISO List of Interconnection Facilities Joint ISO-NE/NYISO document listing the ISO-NE/NYISO Interconnections and description of each of the interties that makes up those interconnections (previously included as a part of the Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff-Attachment F).\n\n#### Attachment G—Procedure to Protect for the Loss of Phase II Imports: (08/30/2010)\n\nThis procedure is designed to prevent the occurrence of a loss of Phase II contingency applicable when Phase II is operated in the isolated or synchronous mode.",

tool_input.query: ISO New England "Large Generating Facility" definition 20 MW Schedule 22 applies

steps.content:
Based on the ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT), Schedule 22 — Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP), effective May 3, 2025 (FERC ER25-2149-000), and the Interconnection Process Guide, here is a comprehensive answer. --- ## Scope: What Is a "Large Generator"? Schedule 22 applies to **Large Generating Facilities**, which are generating facilities **larger than 20 MW**. Facilities ≤20 MW are subject to Schedule 23 (Small Generator Interconnection Procedures). *(Source: FERC Order on Compliance, Docket Nos. ER24-2009-000 and ER24-2007-000, at P 5; ISO-NE Interconnection Service page)* --- ## 1. Prerequisites Before Submitting Before you can submit a valid interconnection request, you must: 1. **Obtain an ISO Customer ID** — New customers must first obtain a customer ID and access to Critical Energy Infrastructure Information (CEII). 2. **Create an IRTT Account** — Register for the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT). 3. **Submit During a Cluster Request Window** — Per FERC Order No. 2023, IRs can only be submitted during a **45-calendar-day Cluster Request Window**. The next window is expected **October 5, 2026 – November 19, 2026**. *(Source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide)* --- ## 2. Required Components of a Valid Interconnection Request Section 3.4 of Schedule 22 (LGIP) governs what constitutes a Valid Interconnection Request. The following must be submitted via IRTT during the Cluster Request Window *(Sources: Schedule 22 LGIP §3.4; Appendix 1 – Interconnection Request form)*: ### A. Completed Interconnection Request Form (Appendix 1) The form requires: | Information Required | Details | |---|---| | **Project Name** | Proposed project identifier | | **Request Type** | Check one: New Large Generating Facility; Capacity increase/modification (potential Material Modification); New wholesale market participation by existing facility; Change from NR to CNR service | | **Interconnection Service Type** | ☐ Network Resource (NR) – energy only
☐ Capacity Network Resource (CNR) – energy + capacity
☐ Option to downgrade to NR if thermal violations found in CNR testing | | **Facility Address/Location** | Town/City, County, and State | | **Point of Interconnection** | Requested POI | | **Type of Facility** | Description | | **Electric Storage** | Whether facility includes storage; whether it charges from the Administered Transmission System; charging and reactive capability specifications; primary frequency response range for storage | | **Fuel Type** | Generating Facility Fuel Type | | **Capacity by Temperature** | Max Gross MW, Max Net MW, and Net MW at POI for temps: ≥90°F, ≥50°F, ≥20°F, ≥0°F | | **Requested Service Levels (MW)** | CNR Summer/Winter; NR Summer/Winter | | **Equipment Configuration** | Description including number of units and GSUs, any control technologies to restrict output | | **Dates** | Requested Commercial Operation Date, Initial Synchronization Date, In-Service Date | | **Site Control Evidence** | See Section 2.C below | | **Customer Information** | Company name, ISO Customer ID, address, representative name/title/contact | | **Authorized Signature** | Signed by an officer of the company | ### B. Required Deposits and Fees The application must be accompanied by deposits. Per Schedule 22 LGIP §3.1 and the ISO-NE Order No. 2023 compliance presentation: | Deposit Type | Amount | Notes | |---|---|---| | **Study Deposit** | **$250,000** (uniform amount per ISO-NE independent entity variation) | Submitted with IR. Consistent with existing New England LGIP requirements. Can be by electronic transfer or irrevocable Letter of Credit from an approved issuer. *(Sources: ISO-NE Order No. 2023 presentation, Sept 2023, slide 14; ISO-NE Transition Q&A, Dec 2023, Q16)* | | **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)** | Required at IR submission (amount as specified per cluster study process) | Must be delivered by electronic transfer or irrevocable Letter of Credit. If LOC is deficient, customer has 5 business days to cure. *(Source: Schedule 22 LGIP §3.1, as reflected in Feb 2024 tariff redlines)* | ### C. Site Control Requirements Per **Schedule 22 LGIP §3.4.2** and **Appendix 1** and the **June 2025 ISO-NE Technical Notice on Regulatory Limitations**: **For Capacity Network Resource (CNR) Interconnection Service:** - **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name is **required — no exceptions**. No deposit-in-lieu option. **For Network Resource (NR) Interconnection Service:** - **Option A:** Provide **100% exclusive Site Control** documentation (fee simple ownership, leasehold, option to purchase, contract to purchase, or applications for permits on federal/state property). - **Option B (if Regulatory Limitations prevent obtaining Site Control):** Provide ALL of the following: 1. **Cash deposit in lieu of Site Control** = **$10,000/MW**, subject to a **minimum of $500,000** and a **maximum of $2,000,000** (refundable within the cure period per §3.4.3) 2. **Signed affidavit from an officer** stating Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations 3. **Documentation** describing and explaining the source and effects of the Regulatory Limitations, including conditions to satisfy them and anticipated timeline **Definition of Regulatory Limitation:** A federal, state, local, or Tribal law that makes it infeasible to obtain Site Control before submitting an IR (e.g., a law requiring a validated ISO-NE IR before obtaining land rights). This does **not** include general permitting or siting issues. *(Source: ISO-NE Technical Notice, June 2, 2025)* **Modifications to existing facilities:** If the proposed modification does not require additional real property, the customer certifies it has Site Control and no additional evidence is needed. ### D. Technical Data Interconnection Customers must submit study-ready technical data as required for the Cluster Study. ISO-NE provides an "Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance" document for this purpose. *(Source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide)* --- ## 3. The ISO Validation Process (Section 3.4.4) Within **5 business days** of receipt, ISO-NE will: - Notify the customer that the IR is valid, **OR** - Identify any deficiencies (including Site Control issues) If deficiencies are identified, the customer has a cure period to remedy them before the Cluster Request Window closes. --- ## 4. Summary of All Required Fees/Deposits Throughout the Process Beyond the initial submission, the LGIP requires escalating financial commitments as the project advances: | Stage | Deposit/ Fee | Amount | |---|---|---| | **IR Submission** | Study Deposit | **$250,000** (uniform) | | **IR Submission** | Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD) | As specified | | **IR Submission (NR only, if no Site Control)** | Site Control Deposit in lieu | **$10,000/MW** (min $500K, max $2M) | | **After Cluster Study Report Meeting** | Additional CRD | **5% of assigned Network Upgrade costs** (within 20 CD) | | **Facilities Study Agreement Execution** | Commercial Readiness Deposit | **Greater of 25% of estimated FS cost or $250,000**, plus proof of **100% Site Control** | | **Interconnection Agreement (LGIA) Execution** | LGIA Deposit | **20% of Network Upgrade costs**, less readiness deposits already on hand | *(Sources: Appendix 3 – Interconnection Facilities Study Agreement §5.0; ISO-NE Oct 2023 cluster study cycle presentation; FERC Order on Compliance ER24-2009-000)* --- ## 5. Key Governing Documents The complete rules are set forth in: - **Schedule 22 of the ISO-NE OATT** — Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (effective 5/3/25) - **ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6 (PP 5-6)** — Scope of Study for Interconnection Studies (effective 10/11/25) - **ISO Operating Procedure No. 14 (OP-14)** — Technical Requirements for Generators - **Appendix 1** – Interconnection Request form - **Appendix 2** – Cluster Study Agreement - **Appendix 3** – Interconnection Facilities Study Agreement - **Appendix 11** – Standard Large Generator Interconnection Agreement (LGIA) All sources: ISO-NE OATT Schedule 22 (iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf); ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide; ISO-NE Appendix 1 IR Form; ISO-NE Technical Notice on Regulatory Limitations (June 2, 2025); FERC Order Docket Nos. ER24-2009-000 and ER24-2007-000 (April 4, 2025).
